question:
Problem A-5
Prove that
\[
\binom{p a}{p b} \equiv\binom{a}{b}(\bmod p)
\]
for all integers \( p, a \), and \( b \) with \( p \) a prime, \( p>0 \), and \( a>b>0 \).
Notation: \( \binom{m}{n} \) denotes the binomial coefficient \( \frac{m!}{n!(m-n)!} \).
solution:
A-5.
It is well known that \( \binom{p}{i} \equiv 0 \bmod p \) for \( i=1,2, \cdots, p-1 \) or equivalently that in \( Z_{p}[x] \) one has \( (1+x)^{p}=1+x^{p} \), where \( Z_{p} \) is the field of the integers modulo \( p \). Thus in \( Z_{p}[x] \),
\[
\sum_{k=0}^{p a}\binom{p a}{k} x^{k}=(1+x)^{p a}=\left[(1+x)^{p}\right]^{a}=\left[1+x^{p}\right]^{a}=\sum_{j=0}^{a}\binom{a}{j} x^{j p} .
\]

Since coefficients of like powers must be congruent modulo \( p \) in the equality
\[
\sum_{k=0}^{p a}\binom{p a}{k} x^{k}=\sum_{j=0}^{c}\binom{a}{j} x^{j p}
\]
in \( Z_{p}[x] \), one sees that
\[
\binom{p a}{p b} \equiv\binom{a}{b}(\bmod p)
\]
for \( b=0,1, \ldots, a \).

descriptive_long.question:
Problem A-5
Prove that
\[
\binom{primevar biggerval}{primevar smallint} \equiv\binom{biggerval}{smallint}(\bmod primevar)
\]
for all integers \( primevar, biggerval \), and \( smallint \) with \( primevar \) a prime, \( primevar>0 \), and \( biggerval>smallint>0 \).
Notation: \( \binom{integerem}{integeren} \) denotes the binomial coefficient \( \frac{integerem!}{integeren!(integerem-integeren)!} \).
descriptive_long.solution:
A-5.
It is well known that \( \binom{primevar}{i} \equiv 0 \\bmod primevar \) for \( i=1,2, \cdots, primevar-1 \) or equivalently that in \( Z_{primevar}[variablex] \) one has \( (1+variablex)^{primevar}=1+variablex^{primevar} \), where \( Z_{primevar} \) is the field of the integers modulo \( primevar \). Thus in \( Z_{primevar}[variablex] \),
\[
\sum_{counterk=0}^{primevar biggerval}\binom{primevar biggerval}{counterk} variablex^{counterk}=(1+variablex)^{primevar biggerval}=\left[(1+variablex)^{primevar}\right]^{biggerval}=\left[1+variablex^{primevar}\right]^{biggerval}=\sum_{indexj=0}^{biggerval}\binom{biggerval}{indexj} variablex^{indexj primevar} .
\]

Since coefficients of like powers must be congruent modulo \( primevar \) in the equality
\[
\sum_{counterk=0}^{primevar biggerval}\binom{primevar biggerval}{counterk} variablex^{counterk}=\sum_{indexj=0}^{charparam}\binom{biggerval}{indexj} variablex^{indexj primevar}
\]
in \( Z_{primevar}[variablex] \), one sees that
\[
\binom{primevar biggerval}{primevar smallint} \equiv\binom{biggerval}{smallint}(\bmod primevar)
\]
for \( smallint=0,1, \ldots, biggerval \).

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Problem A-5
Prove that
\[
\binom{lanternbloom driftwooden}{lanternbloom starlitridge} \equiv\binom{driftwooden}{starlitridge}(\bmod lanternbloom)
\]
for all integers \( lanternbloom, driftwooden \), and \( starlitridge \) with \( lanternbloom \) a prime, \( lanternbloom>0 \), and \( driftwooden>starlitridge>0 \).
Notation: \( \binom{cloudanchor}{pebbletrail} \) denotes the binomial coefficient \( \frac{cloudanchor!}{pebbletrail!(cloudanchor-pebbletrail)!} \).
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
A-5.
It is well known that \( \binom{lanternbloom}{i} \equiv 0 \bmod lanternbloom \) for \( i=1,2, \cdots, lanternbloom-1 \) or equivalently that in \( Z_{lanternbloom}[quillsparrow] \) one has \( (1+quillsparrow)^{lanternbloom}=1+quillsparrow^{lanternbloom} \), where \( Z_{lanternbloom} \) is the field of the integers modulo \( lanternbloom \). Thus in \( Z_{lanternbloom}[quillsparrow] \),
\[
\sum_{thimbleweed=0}^{lanternbloom driftwooden}\binom{lanternbloom driftwooden}{thimbleweed} quillsparrow^{thimbleweed}=(1+quillsparrow)^{lanternbloom driftwooden}=\left[(1+quillsparrow)^{lanternbloom}\right]^{driftwooden}=\left[1+quillsparrow^{lanternbloom}\right]^{driftwooden}=\sum_{butterglory=0}^{driftwooden}\binom{driftwooden}{butterglory} quillsparrow^{butterglory lanternbloom} .
\]

Since coefficients of like powers must be congruent modulo \( lanternbloom \) in the equality
\[
\sum_{thimbleweed=0}^{lanternbloom driftwooden}\binom{lanternbloom driftwooden}{thimbleweed} quillsparrow^{thimbleweed}=\sum_{butterglory=0}^{moonlilac}\binom{driftwooden}{butterglory} quillsparrow^{butterglory lanternbloom}
\]
in \( Z_{lanternbloom}[quillsparrow] \), one sees that
\[
\binom{lanternbloom driftwooden}{lanternbloom starlitridge} \equiv\binom{driftwooden}{starlitridge}(\bmod lanternbloom)
\]
for \( starlitridge=0,1, \ldots, driftwooden \).

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Problem A-5
Prove that
\[
\binom{composite terminal}{composite external} \equiv\binom{terminal}{external}(\bmod composite)
\]
for all integers \( composite, terminal \), and \( external \) with \( composite \) a prime, \( composite>0 \), and \( terminal>external>0 \).
Notation: \( \binom{constant}{immutable} \) denotes the binomial coefficient \( \frac{constant!}{immutable!(constant-immutable)!} \).
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
A-5.
It is well known that \( \binom{composite}{unindexed} \equiv 0 \bmod composite \) for \( unindexed=1,2, \cdots, composite-1 \) or equivalently that in \( Z_{composite}[steadystate] \) one has \( (1+steadystate)^{composite}=1+steadystate^{composite} \), where \( Z_{composite} \) is the field of the integers modulo \( composite \). Thus in \( Z_{composite}[steadystate] \),
\[
\sum_{singlevalue=0}^{composite\ terminal}\binom{composite\ terminal}{singlevalue} steadystate^{singlevalue}=(1+steadystate)^{composite\ terminal}=\left[(1+steadystate)^{composite}\right]^{terminal}=\left[1+steadystate^{composite}\right]^{terminal}=\sum_{fixedpoint=0}^{terminal}\binom{terminal}{fixedpoint} steadystate^{fixedpoint\ composite} .
\]

Since coefficients of like powers must be congruent modulo \( composite \) in the equality
\[
\sum_{singlevalue=0}^{composite\ terminal}\binom{composite\ terminal}{singlevalue} steadystate^{singlevalue}=\sum_{fixedpoint=0}^{undefined}\binom{terminal}{fixedpoint} steadystate^{fixedpoint\ composite}
\]
in \( Z_{composite}[steadystate] \), one sees that
\[
\binom{composite\ terminal}{composite\ external} \equiv\binom{terminal}{external}(\bmod composite)
\]
for \( external=0,1, \ldots, terminal \).

garbled_string.question:
Problem A-5
Prove that
\[
\binom{qzxwvtnp hjgrksla}{qzxwvtnp vkdplqtw} \equiv\binom{hjgrksla}{vkdplqtw}(\bmod qzxwvtnp)
\]
for all integers \( qzxwvtnp, hjgrksla \), and \( vkdplqtw \) with \( qzxwvtnp \) a prime, \( qzxwvtnp>0 \), and \( hjgrksla>vkdplqtw>0 \).
Notation: \( \binom{xjkwprem}{uvyfteop} \) denotes the binomial coefficient \( \frac{xjkwprem!}{uvyfteop!(xjkwprem-uvyfteop)!} \).
garbled_string.solution:
A-5.
It is well known that \( \binom{qzxwvtnp}{bvncmars} \equiv 0 \bmod qzxwvtnp \) for \( bvncmars=1,2, \cdots, qzxwvtnp-1 \) or equivalently that in \( Z_{qzxwvtnp}[rlkdpaqv] \) one has \( (1+rlkdpaqv)^{qzxwvtnp}=1+rlkdpaqv^{qzxwvtnp} \), where \( Z_{qzxwvtnp} \) is the field of the integers modulo \( qzxwvtnp \). Thus in \( Z_{qzxwvtnp}[rlkdpaqv] \),
\[
\sum_{tzlrqwish=0}^{qzxwvtnp hjgrksla}\binom{qzxwvtnp hjgrksla}{tzlrqwish} rlkdpaqv^{tzlrqwish}=(1+rlkdpaqv)^{qzxwvtnp hjgrksla}=\left[(1+rlkdpaqv)^{qzxwvtnp}\right]^{hjgrksla}=\left[1+rlkdpaqv^{qzxwvtnp}\right]^{hjgrksla}=\sum_{soccusdn=0}^{hjgrksla}\binom{hjgrksla}{soccusdn} rlkdpaqv^{soccusdn qzxwvtnp} .
\]

Since coefficients of like powers must be congruent modulo \( qzxwvtnp \) in the equality
\[
\sum_{tzlrqwish=0}^{qzxwvtnp hjgrksla}\binom{qzxwvtnp hjgrksla}{tzlrqwish} rlkdpaqv^{tzlrqwish}=\sum_{soccusdn=0}^{fmszwxne}\binom{hjgrksla}{soccusdn} rlkdpaqv^{soccusdn qzxwvtnp}
\]
in \( Z_{qzxwvtnp}[rlkdpaqv] \), one sees that
\[
\binom{qzxwvtnp hjgrksla}{qzxwvtnp vkdplqtw} \equiv\binom{hjgrksla}{vkdplqtw}(\bmod qzxwvtnp)
\]
for \( vkdplqtw=0,1, \ldots, hjgrksla \).

kernel_variant.question:
(Ljunggren-Jacobsthal super-congruence --- sharp exponent, block-multinomial extension)

Fix a prime number $p\ge 5$ and integers $r,s$ with $r\ge s\ge 0$.

A.  (Jacobsthal-Ljunggren modulo $p^{3}$)  
Show that  
\[
\binom{p\,r}{p\,s}\equiv\binom{r}{s}\pmod{p^{3}}. \tag{1}
\]

B.  (How large can the modulus be?)

1.  Prove that  
\[
v_{p}\!\Bigl(\binom{2p}{p}-2\Bigr)=
3+v_{p}\!\bigl(B_{p-3}\bigr), \tag{2}
\]
where $B_{k}$ denotes the $k$-th Bernoulli number and $v_{p}$ is
the $p$-adic valuation.

2.  Put $\lambda:=v_{p}(B_{p-3})$.

   (a)  Show that  
   \[
   v_{p}\!\Bigl(\binom{2p}{p}-2\Bigr)=3+\lambda .
   \]

   (b)  Deduce that for the pair $(r,s)=(2,1)$ the largest exponent
   $E=E(p)$ for which  
   \[
   \binom{p\,r}{p\,s}\equiv\binom{r}{s}\pmod{p^{E}}
   \]
   holds is precisely $E=3+\lambda$.  
   Conclude in particular

   *  If $\lambda=0$ (equivalently $p\nmid B_{p-3}$, the generic
   case) then the modulus $p^{3}$ in (1) is optimal.

   *  If $p$ is a Wolstenholme prime (so that $\lambda\ge 1$) the
   modulus improves to $p^{4}$ or higher according as
   $\lambda=1,2,\dots$, and the optimal exponent is $3+\lambda$.
   In particular, for the two currently known Wolstenholme primes
   one has $\lambda=1$ and the best modulus is $p^{4}$.

C.  ($d$-fold block-multinomial version)  
Let a fixed integer $d\ge 2$ be given.  
For vectors
\[
\mathbf r=(r_{1},\dots ,r_{d}),\quad
\mathbf s=(s_{1},\dots ,s_{d}),\qquad
0\le s_{i}\le r_{i},\;\;
\sum_{i=1}^{d}r_{i}=r,\;
\sum_{i=1}^{d}s_{i}=s,
\]
write $\alpha_{i}:=r_{i}-s_{i}$ and set
\[
M_{p}(\mathbf r,\mathbf s):=\frac{(p\,r)!}{\displaystyle\prod_{i=1}^{d}(p\,s_{i})!\,(p\,\alpha_{i})!},
\qquad
M_{1}(\mathbf r,\mathbf s):=\frac{r!}{\displaystyle\prod_{i=1}^{d}s_{i}!\,\alpha_{i}!}.
\]
Prove the super-congruence  
\[
M_{p}(\mathbf r,\mathbf s)\equiv M_{1}(\mathbf r,\mathbf s)\pmod{p^{3}}. \tag{3}
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout $p\ge 5$ is fixed, $v_{p}$ denotes the $p$-adic valuation, and  
\[
H_{n,m}:=\sum_{k=1}^{n}\frac1{k^{m}},\qquad H_{n}:=H_{n,1}.
\]
Bernoulli numbers are defined by  
\[
\frac{t}{e^{t}-1}=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}B_{k}\frac{t^{k}}{k!}.
\]
We shall make repeated use of  

(F1)  (Wolstenholme) $H_{p-1}\equiv 0\pmod{p^{2}}$,

(F2)  $H_{p-1,2}\equiv 0\pmod{p}$,

(F3)  $\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{p-1}k^{m}\equiv 0\pmod{p}$ whenever
$p-1\nmid m$,

and of the classical congruences due to Glaisher  

(G1)  $H_{p-1}\equiv-\dfrac{p^{2}}{3}B_{p-3}\pmod{p^{3}}$,

(G2)  $H_{p-1,2}\equiv\dfrac{2p}{3}B_{p-3}\pmod{p^{2}}$.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
A.  Proof of (1)

Define  
\[
F(r,s):=\frac{\binom{p\,r}{p\,s}}{\binom{r}{s}},\qquad r\ge s.
\]

Step 1.  Block decomposition.  
Writing the factorials in blocks of length $p$ one obtains the exact
equality  
\[
\frac{\binom{p\,r}{p\,s}}{\binom{r}{s}}
    =\prod_{j=1}^{s}\prod_{t=1}^{p-1}
      \frac{p(r-s)+p(j-1)+t}{p(j-1)+t}. \tag{4}
\]

Put  
\[
x_{j,t}:=\frac{p(r-s)}{p(j-1)+t},\qquad |x_{j,t}|_{p}=p^{-1}.
\]
Because $|x_{j,t}|_{p}<1$, the $p$-adic logarithm converges, giving
\[
\log F(r,s)=
\sum_{m\ge 1}\frac{(-1)^{m+1}}{m}\,
            p^{m}(r-s)^{m}
            \sum_{j=1}^{s}\sum_{t=1}^{p-1}(p(j-1)+t)^{-m}. \tag{5}
\]

Step 2.  Valuation of the inner sums.  
Set  
\[
S_{m}(s):=\sum_{u=0}^{s-1}\sum_{t=1}^{p-1}(p\,u+t)^{-m}.
\]

Lemma 1.  One has $v_{p}\!\bigl(S_{1}(s)\bigr)\ge 2$ and
$v_{p}\!\bigl(S_{2}(s)\bigr)\ge 1$.

Proof.  
Expand $(p\,u+t)^{-1}$ in a geometric series and use (F1)-(F2); the
details are identical to the original solution. \blacksquare 

Step 3.  Finishing the valuation.  
From (5) and Lemma 1 one finds that every term in the series for
$\log F(r,s)$ has $p$-adic valuation at least $3$, hence
\[
v_{p}\bigl(\log F(r,s)\bigr)\ge 3,\qquad
\log F(r,s)\equiv 0\pmod{p^{3}}.
\]
Because $p^{3}\mid\log F(r,s)$ we may truncate the $p$-adic
exponential and conclude $F(r,s)\equiv 1\pmod{p^{3}}$, i.e. (1). \blacksquare 



--------------------------------------------------------------------
B.  Sharpness of the exponent

B.1  Exact valuation of $\displaystyle\binom{2p}{p}-2$

Set  
\[
G:=\frac12\binom{2p}{p}
     =\binom{2p-1}{\,p-1\,}
     =\prod_{k=1}^{p-1}\Bigl(1+\frac{p}{k}\Bigr). \tag{6}
\]
Taking the $p$-adic logarithm and expanding as in (5) gives  
\[
\log G=
p\,H_{p-1}-\frac{p^{2}}{2}H_{p-1,2}
+\frac{p^{3}}{3}H_{p-1,3}
-\bigl(\text{terms divisible by }p^{4}\bigr). \tag{7}
\]
Insert (G1)-(G2) and use $p\mid H_{p-1,3}$ (from (F3)):
\[
\log G\equiv
-\frac{2}{3}\,p^{3}B_{p-3}\pmod{p^{4}}. \tag{8}
\]
Because $p^{4}\mid(\log G)^{2}$, the $p$-adic exponential truncates
after the linear term:
\[
G=\exp(\log G)\equiv
1-\frac{2}{3}\,p^{3}B_{p-3}\pmod{p^{4}}. \tag{9}
\]
Multiplying by $2$ yields
\[
\binom{2p}{p}\equiv
2-\frac{4}{3}\,p^{3}B_{p-3}\pmod{p^{4}}. \tag{10}
\]
Since $-\dfrac43$ is a $p$-adic unit,
\[
v_{p}\!\Bigl(\binom{2p}{p}-2\Bigr)=3+v_{p}\!\bigl(B_{p-3}\bigr),
\]
establishing (2). \blacksquare 



B.2  Consequences for the optimal exponent  

Let $\lambda:=v_{p}(B_{p-3})$; then by (2)
\[
v_{p}\!\Bigl(\binom{2p}{p}-2\Bigr)=3+\lambda . \tag{11}
\]

For the pair $(r,s)=(2,1)$ we have  
\[
\binom{p\,r}{p\,s}-\binom{r}{s}=\binom{2p}{p}-2,
\]
so (11) gives
\[
v_{p}\!\Bigl(\binom{p\,r}{p\,s}-\binom{r}{s}\Bigr)=3+\lambda .
\]
Therefore

*  $\displaystyle\binom{p\,r}{p\,s}\equiv\binom{r}{s}\pmod{p^{3+\lambda}}$,  

*  $\displaystyle\binom{p\,r}{p\,s}\not\equiv\binom{r}{s}\pmod{p^{4+\lambda}}$.

Hence the largest exponent for which the congruence (1) can hold in
general is $E=3+\lambda$.

Special cases.

(i)  Generic primes ($\lambda=0$).  
Then $E=3$, so the modulus $p^{3}$ in (1) is best possible.

(ii)  Wolstenholme primes ($\lambda\ge 1$).  
The modulus improves to $p^{4}$ or higher; its exact value is
$p^{3+\lambda}$.  
For the two known Wolstenholme primes $16843$ and $2124679$ one has
$\lambda=1$, whence the optimal exponent is $4$. \blacksquare 



--------------------------------------------------------------------
C.  Proof of the block-multinomial congruence (3)

Step 1.  Iterated-binomial decomposition.  
Introduce
\[
k_{2i-1}:=s_{i},\qquad
k_{2i}:=\alpha_{i}=r_{i}-s_{i}\quad(1\le i\le d),\qquad
m:=2d,
\]
and partial sums $L_{j}:=\sum_{i=1}^{j-1}k_{i}$ ($L_{1}=0$).  The
classical identity
\[
\binom{n}{k_{1},\dots ,k_{m}}=\prod_{j=1}^{m-1}\binom{n-L_{j}}{k_{j}}
\tag{12}
\]
gives
\[
M_{1}(\mathbf r,\mathbf s)
      =\prod_{j=1}^{m-1}\binom{r-L_{j}}{k_{j}},\qquad
M_{p}(\mathbf r,\mathbf s)
      =\prod_{j=1}^{m-1}\binom{p\,r-p\,L_{j}}{p\,k_{j}}. \tag{13}
\]

Step 2.  Application of Part A.  
For every $j$ one has $r-L_{j}\ge k_{j}\ge 0$, hence (1) yields  
\[
\binom{p(r-L_{j})}{p\,k_{j}}\equiv
\binom{r-L_{j}}{k_{j}}\pmod{p^{3}}.
\]
Dividing (13) by (12) we get
\[
\frac{M_{p}(\mathbf r,\mathbf s)}{M_{1}(\mathbf r,\mathbf s)}
      =\prod_{j=1}^{m-1}\bigl(1+p^{3}\theta_{j}\bigr)
      \equiv 1\pmod{p^{3}}, \tag{14}
\]
with $\theta_{j}\in\mathbb Z_{p}$.  Multiplying by
$M_{1}(\mathbf r,\mathbf s)$ proves (3). \blacksquare 



--------------------------------------------------------------------

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1.  The original problem required only a first-level Freshman-Dream argument modulo p.  
    The enhanced variant asks for a congruence two orders deeper (mod p³) and is known classically as Ljunggren’s theorem; its proof forces the solver to manipulate p-adic logarithms/ exponentials or, equivalently, to perform delicate denominator–sums that do not appear at all in the original exercise.

2.  Optimality (part B) obliges the contestant not only to prove a congruence but also to understand its exact strength, constructing explicit counter-examples beyond p³.  This uses detailed expansions of binomial quotients and harmonic sums mod high powers of p.

3.  Part C couples the higher-order binomial congruence with a multidimensional generalisation, intertwining multinomial coefficients and the earlier p-adic analysis.  Handling several interacting indices simultaneously and showing that all error terms cancel demands an even subtler bookkeeping of congruences.

4.  Techniques needed now include  
   • $p$-adic valuations and carries (Kummer theory),  
   • $p$-adic logarithm/exponential series,  
   • harmonic-sum congruences (Wolstenholme type), and  
   • factorisations of multinomial quotients,  
all of which are far beyond the elementary polynomial manipulation sufficient for the original problem.  
Thus the new kernel variant is substantially, demonstrably harder.

original_kernel_variant.question:
(Ljunggren-Jacobsthal super-congruence --- sharp exponent, block-multinomial extension)

Fix a prime number $p\ge 5$ and integers $r,s$ with $r\ge s\ge 0$.

where $B_{k}$ denotes the $k$-th Bernoulli number and $v_{p}$ is

   *  If $\lambda=0$ (equivalently $p\nmid B_{p-3}$, the generic

Let a fixed integer $d\ge 2$ be given.  
For vectors

\mathbf r=(r_{1},\dots ,r_{d}),\quad

\[
M_{p}(\mathbf r,\mathbf s)\equiv M_{1}(\mathbf r,\mathbf s)\pmod{p^{3}}. \tag{3}
\]

original_kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout $p\ge 5$ is fixed, $v_{p}$ denotes the $p$-adic valuation, and  
\[
H_{n,m}:=\sum_{k=1}^{n}\frac1{k^{m}},\qquad H_{n}:=H_{n,1}.
\]
Bernoulli numbers are defined by  
\[
\frac{t}{e^{t}-1}=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}B_{k}\frac{t^{k}}{k!}.
\]
We shall make repeated use of  

(F1)  (Wolstenholme) $H_{p-1}\equiv 0\pmod{p^{2}}$,

(F2)  $H_{p-1,2}\equiv 0\pmod{p}$,

(F3)  $\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{p-1}k^{m}\equiv 0\pmod{p}$ whenever
$p-1\nmid m$,

and of the classical congruences due to Glaisher  

(G1)  $H_{p-1}\equiv-\dfrac{p^{2}}{3}B_{p-3}\pmod{p^{3}}$,

(G2)  $H_{p-1,2}\equiv\dfrac{2p}{3}B_{p-3}\pmod{p^{2}}$.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
A.  Proof of (1)

Define  
\[
F(r,s):=\frac{\binom{p\,r}{p\,s}}{\binom{r}{s}},\qquad r\ge s.
\]

Step 1.  Block decomposition.  
Writing the factorials in blocks of length $p$ one obtains the exact
equality  
\[
\frac{\binom{p\,r}{p\,s}}{\binom{r}{s}}
    =\prod_{j=1}^{s}\prod_{t=1}^{p-1}
      \frac{p(r-s)+p(j-1)+t}{p(j-1)+t}. \tag{4}
\]

Put  
\[
x_{j,t}:=\frac{p(r-s)}{p(j-1)+t},\qquad |x_{j,t}|_{p}=p^{-1}.
\]
Because $|x_{j,t}|_{p}<1$, the $p$-adic logarithm converges, giving
\[
\log F(r,s)=
\sum_{m\ge 1}\frac{(-1)^{m+1}}{m}\,
            p^{m}(r-s)^{m}
            \sum_{j=1}^{s}\sum_{t=1}^{p-1}(p(j-1)+t)^{-m}. \tag{5}
\]

Step 2.  Valuation of the inner sums.  
Set  
\[
S_{m}(s):=\sum_{u=0}^{s-1}\sum_{t=1}^{p-1}(p\,u+t)^{-m}.
\]

Lemma 1.  One has $v_{p}\!\bigl(S_{1}(s)\bigr)\ge 2$ and
$v_{p}\!\bigl(S_{2}(s)\bigr)\ge 1$.

Proof.  
Expand $(p\,u+t)^{-1}$ in a geometric series and use (F1)-(F2); the
details are identical to the original solution. \blacksquare 

Step 3.  Finishing the valuation.  
From (5) and Lemma 1 one finds that every term in the series for
$\log F(r,s)$ has $p$-adic valuation at least $3$, hence
\[
v_{p}\bigl(\log F(r,s)\bigr)\ge 3,\qquad
\log F(r,s)\equiv 0\pmod{p^{3}}.
\]
Because $p^{3}\mid\log F(r,s)$ we may truncate the $p$-adic
exponential and conclude $F(r,s)\equiv 1\pmod{p^{3}}$, i.e. (1). \blacksquare 



--------------------------------------------------------------------
B.  Sharpness of the exponent

B.1  Exact valuation of $\displaystyle\binom{2p}{p}-2$

Set  
\[
G:=\frac12\binom{2p}{p}
     =\binom{2p-1}{\,p-1\,}
     =\prod_{k=1}^{p-1}\Bigl(1+\frac{p}{k}\Bigr). \tag{6}
\]
Taking the $p$-adic logarithm and expanding as in (5) gives  
\[
\log G=
p\,H_{p-1}-\frac{p^{2}}{2}H_{p-1,2}
+\frac{p^{3}}{3}H_{p-1,3}
-\bigl(\text{terms divisible by }p^{4}\bigr). \tag{7}
\]
Insert (G1)-(G2) and use $p\mid H_{p-1,3}$ (from (F3)):
\[
\log G\equiv
-\frac{2}{3}\,p^{3}B_{p-3}\pmod{p^{4}}. \tag{8}
\]
Because $p^{4}\mid(\log G)^{2}$, the $p$-adic exponential truncates
after the linear term:
\[
G=\exp(\log G)\equiv
1-\frac{2}{3}\,p^{3}B_{p-3}\pmod{p^{4}}. \tag{9}
\]
Multiplying by $2$ yields
\[
\binom{2p}{p}\equiv
2-\frac{4}{3}\,p^{3}B_{p-3}\pmod{p^{4}}. \tag{10}
\]
Since $-\dfrac43$ is a $p$-adic unit,
\[
v_{p}\!\Bigl(\binom{2p}{p}-2\Bigr)=3+v_{p}\!\bigl(B_{p-3}\bigr),
\]
establishing (2). \blacksquare 



B.2  Consequences for the optimal exponent  

Let $\lambda:=v_{p}(B_{p-3})$; then by (2)
\[
v_{p}\!\Bigl(\binom{2p}{p}-2\Bigr)=3+\lambda . \tag{11}
\]

For the pair $(r,s)=(2,1)$ we have  
\[
\binom{p\,r}{p\,s}-\binom{r}{s}=\binom{2p}{p}-2,
\]
so (11) gives
\[
v_{p}\!\Bigl(\binom{p\,r}{p\,s}-\binom{r}{s}\Bigr)=3+\lambda .
\]
Therefore

*  $\displaystyle\binom{p\,r}{p\,s}\equiv\binom{r}{s}\pmod{p^{3+\lambda}}$,  

*  $\displaystyle\binom{p\,r}{p\,s}\not\equiv\binom{r}{s}\pmod{p^{4+\lambda}}$.

Hence the largest exponent for which the congruence (1) can hold in
general is $E=3+\lambda$.

Special cases.

(i)  Generic primes ($\lambda=0$).  
Then $E=3$, so the modulus $p^{3}$ in (1) is best possible.

(ii)  Wolstenholme primes ($\lambda\ge 1$).  
The modulus improves to $p^{4}$ or higher; its exact value is
$p^{3+\lambda}$.  
For the two known Wolstenholme primes $16843$ and $2124679$ one has
$\lambda=1$, whence the optimal exponent is $4$. \blacksquare 



--------------------------------------------------------------------
C.  Proof of the block-multinomial congruence (3)

Step 1.  Iterated-binomial decomposition.  
Introduce
\[
k_{2i-1}:=s_{i},\qquad
k_{2i}:=\alpha_{i}=r_{i}-s_{i}\quad(1\le i\le d),\qquad
m:=2d,
\]
and partial sums $L_{j}:=\sum_{i=1}^{j-1}k_{i}$ ($L_{1}=0$).  The
classical identity
\[
\binom{n}{k_{1},\dots ,k_{m}}=\prod_{j=1}^{m-1}\binom{n-L_{j}}{k_{j}}
\tag{12}
\]
gives
\[
M_{1}(\mathbf r,\mathbf s)
      =\prod_{j=1}^{m-1}\binom{r-L_{j}}{k_{j}},\qquad
M_{p}(\mathbf r,\mathbf s)
      =\prod_{j=1}^{m-1}\binom{p\,r-p\,L_{j}}{p\,k_{j}}. \tag{13}
\]

Step 2.  Application of Part A.  
For every $j$ one has $r-L_{j}\ge k_{j}\ge 0$, hence (1) yields  
\[
\binom{p(r-L_{j})}{p\,k_{j}}\equiv
\binom{r-L_{j}}{k_{j}}\pmod{p^{3}}.
\]
Dividing (13) by (12) we get
\[
\frac{M_{p}(\mathbf r,\mathbf s)}{M_{1}(\mathbf r,\mathbf s)}
      =\prod_{j=1}^{m-1}\bigl(1+p^{3}\theta_{j}\bigr)
      \equiv 1\pmod{p^{3}}, \tag{14}
\]
with $\theta_{j}\in\mathbb Z_{p}$.  Multiplying by
$M_{1}(\mathbf r,\mathbf s)$ proves (3). \blacksquare 



--------------------------------------------------------------------

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1.  The original problem required only a first-level Freshman-Dream argument modulo p.  
    The enhanced variant asks for a congruence two orders deeper (mod p³) and is known classically as Ljunggren’s theorem; its proof forces the solver to manipulate p-adic logarithms/ exponentials or, equivalently, to perform delicate denominator–sums that do not appear at all in the original exercise.

2.  Optimality (part B) obliges the contestant not only to prove a congruence but also to understand its exact strength, constructing explicit counter-examples beyond p³.  This uses detailed expansions of binomial quotients and harmonic sums mod high powers of p.

3.  Part C couples the higher-order binomial congruence with a multidimensional generalisation, intertwining multinomial coefficients and the earlier p-adic analysis.  Handling several interacting indices simultaneously and showing that all error terms cancel demands an even subtler bookkeeping of congruences.

4.  Techniques needed now include  
   • $p$-adic valuations and carries (Kummer theory),  
   • $p$-adic logarithm/exponential series,  
   • harmonic-sum congruences (Wolstenholme type), and  
   • factorisations of multinomial quotients,  
all of which are far beyond the elementary polynomial manipulation sufficient for the original problem.  
Thus the new kernel variant is substantially, demonstrably harder.
